topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of lavender and old paper hung heavy in Dr. Elias Thorne’s office, a stark contrast to the rough playground dust I remembered. I sat on the edge of the leather armchair, my hands twisting the frayed edge of my sweater. Across the mahogany desk sat Marcus. He looked… different. The sharp angles of his jaw were softened by a kind of weary patience, and the familiar sneer I’d carried for years was replaced by a gentle, almost sad understanding in his eyes. “So,” he began, his voice a low, even murmur, “tell me about the summer of ’98.” My throat tightened. I swallowed hard, the movement visible in my slender neck. The memory of his shove, the way the laughter of the other kids had felt like physical blows, rose up, hot and stinging behind my eyes. I didn’t look at him. I focused instead on the intricate pattern of the rug beneath my feet, trying to anchor myself against the sudden, dizzying vulnerability of recounting something so raw to the very person who used to make my skin crawl.

[story 2]

I fidgeted constantly. My fingers drummed a frantic rhythm on the armrest, a silent language betraying the churning in my stomach. Marcus, the boy who had been my tormentor since kindergarten, now wore a tailored tweed jacket and possessed an air of profound calm. He leaned forward, resting his elbows on the desk, his gaze steady and unwavering. “It takes a lot of courage to unpack those early experiences,” he observed, his tone utterly devoid of judgment. A tremor ran through my hands, and I quickly pulled them into my lap, wishing I could disappear into the upholstery. I tried to speak, but the words caught, snagged in my chest like barbed wire. “I… I don’t know what to say,” I managed, my voice thin and reedy. The silence stretched, thick and suffocating. I felt a prickling behind my eyes, a desperate urge to look away, to retreat entirely from the intensity of his presence. It was terrifying to be this exposed, this unguarded, in front of him.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the waiting room hummed, a high, irritating whine that mirrored the tension coiled inside me. When Marcus finally called my name, I rose slowly, every movement feeling overly deliberate, as if I were navigating a minefield. He greeted me with a small, almost imperceptible nod, and the simple acknowledgment sent a jolt through me—a confusing mix of old fear and something fragile, something unexpectedly tender. Inside the consultation room, the air felt charged. I watched his hands as he took notes, noting the slight furrow in his brow when I described a particularly painful incident. My breathing hitched, shallow and rapid. Every word I spoke felt weighted, scrutinized. I kept my gaze fixed on a point just above his shoulder, my jaw tight, trying desperately to keep the tremor from escalating into a full-blown shake. I was acutely aware of every muscle in my body, every flicker of expression, hoping that by controlling my physical reactions, I could somehow control the overwhelming flood of feeling bubbling just beneath the surface.
